# Functional

<!-- AUTO-INDEX:START (generated by `python -m engram topics` — do not edit by hand) -->
## Atom index (44 atoms, auto-generated from atoms.jsonl)

_Every atom under this topic with its source proof link. Regenerate with `python -m engram topics`._

### claim (11)
- **These concepts lie on a spectrum between the math of category theory / lambda calculus and their specific mapping to each language's implementation.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **What guarantees the same output forever is having no side effects and no state.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **A pure function contains nothing inside it that isn't directly derived from the input.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Once you introduce state, the outcome of a function can vary based on that state.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **In pure FP there are no objects or structures in memory that you mutate to track what's going on.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **You're not allowed to mutate variables: once you set a value you cannot change it again.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **Conventional iteration loops (for and while) are impossible in pure FP because they require mutating a variable that tracks how many times you've iterated.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Recursion is just a fancier form of iteration.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **This find-in-array pattern recurs everywhere; the only difference between such functions is the condition in the if statement.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Manual currying is an older approach; you can automate it in JavaScript and many languages using bind.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **These functional fundamentals (filter, map, take) are built directly into JavaScript.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### concept (2)
- **The userID acts as a secret extra parameter bound to the returned function, which remembers that value and uses it later.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Chaining filter, map, and take creates a data pipeline: the code declares WHAT should happen while the HOW stays hidden in the fundamental functions.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`

### definition (6)
- **A function is a pure, elegant thing that takes an input and gives an output, and the same input always gives the same output.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **Functional programming takes one extreme of the state question: there shall be no state.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **The passed-in condition function is called a predicate; the generalized function becomes a generic filter.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Currying: instead of handling the entire operation at once, return a function that handles the next part of the operation with the previous state bound to it.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **map is a function that takes a function giving the desired result for each element and inserts that transformed element into the new array.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **take(count) gives you up to 'count' elements from the array, and is chainable.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### method (15)
- **You transform inputs into outputs by composing and connecting functions — hooking inputs and outputs into one long chain that achieves the program's goal.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **To loop functionally, pull the part you want to repeat out into its own function.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Replicate the for loop's index increment by creating a new variable (next index) instead of mutating index, since you can't mutate.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Repeat by having the function call itself recursively with the next index.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **The for loop's condition maps to: only execute the body if the condition (index still in range) is met, which breaks the recursion so all the functions return.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **The for loop's starting point maps to the first call into the recursive loop.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **To return matching results, build an array with the current item at the front and the results of the deeper recursive calls appended to the end, preserving order.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Only append the current item if it matches the condition; otherwise pass through the previous result unchanged; base case returns empty when the list is fully processed.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Replace the three near-identical functions with one by passing in a function that returns whether an item should be included.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **Solution: have the function take only the userID and construct and return a new function that takes a receipt and checks it; pass that returned function into filter.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **The modern preferred way is the lambda function: move the check right into an inline function.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **Use the built-in array map with a lambda to transform each receipt into a result object holding the receipt, final transaction, and discrepancy.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Convert skip-over logic into a filter by inverting the conditions so they describe what to keep instead of what to ignore.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **sort takes a comparator returning negative if a<b and positive if a>b; subtracting b from a sorts ascending, so subtract a from b to order biggest-first.** (negative if a<b; positive if a>b) — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Use slice to take only the count number of elements at the end of the pipeline.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### result (1)
- **The whole procedural routine, rewritten as a functional pipeline, fits in only 12 lines.** (12 lines) — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### example (3)
- **With lambdas you can filter by user, filter by merchant, or do a more complex check such as the total being in a range.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Worked example goal: grab each receipt, look up its transaction, filter unsettled or mismatched ones, sort by the most egregious discrepancy, take the top 10, and display them in a dashboard.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **A satirical fake-startup ad ('Black Sky Financial') frames the receipts demo — a parody company that sues mom-and-pop restaurants over tip discrepancies.** _(hypothetical)_ — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### caveat (3)
- **Without a stopping condition the recursion calls forever until it goes beyond the array size and throws an error.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **Problem: the userID-equals function needs two parameters (receipt and userID) but filter only passes the receipt, so the userID to compare against is unknown.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **After deriving the functional fundamentals by hand, you can remove unneeded variables to make the code more concise.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`

### opinion (3)
- **Functional programming is awesome, but functional programmers are bad marketers — jargon like currying, monads, and binding functors confuses people.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[CORE]`
- **The functional answer to 'how do you loop' is usually 'more functions' — when all you have is a hammer.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
- **It's ironic that FP is sold as so different from OOP, yet binding data this way creates an instance of a function with bound data — the mirror image of an OOP instance of data with bound functions.** — [nuML9SmdbJ4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nuML9SmdbJ4) `[STATED]`
